name: google-ad-rfp-software
prompt: Write me a couple of responsive search ad headlines and descriptions for our RFP-response automation software. We sell to proposal managers at mid-size consultancies.
expected_output: Ready-to-use RSA assets: multiple distinct headlines under 30 characters and descriptions under 90 characters, benefit-led and aimed at proposal managers, with one clear CTA.
assertions: Produces actual ad copy (headlines and descriptions), not a description of what to write
Provides at least 5 headlines, each at or under ~30 characters
Provides at least 2 descriptions, each at or under ~90 characters
At least one headline or description leads with a reader benefit or outcome (e.g. time saved, more wins), not just a product feature
Includes at least one clear call to action
Uses no fabricated statistics; any specific metric is a marked placeholder

name: messaging-hierarchy-wait
prompt: We just rebranded our developer-tooling startup and I need a messaging hierarchy — the primary message, supporting pillars, the works. Can you put one together?
expected_output: Recognizes a messaging hierarchy as a load-bearing strategy statement and asks 2-3 sharp scoping questions (audience, key differentiator, main alternative) before writing anything.
assertions: Asks 2-3 scoping questions before producing the hierarchy
At least one question targets the specific target audience
At least one question targets the key differentiator or the main alternative/competitor
Does NOT output a full messaging hierarchy built on guessed inputs in this turn
Questions are concise (not an open-ended interrogation of many items)

name: content-pillars-podcast
prompt: I run a podcast about indie game development and want to build out content pillars so our YouTube and newsletter stop feeling random. Give me a structure.
expected_output: A concrete content-pillar structure (3-5 themed pillars with example topics and channel mapping) delivered on stated assumptions, not a request for more info.
assertions: Produces an actual content-pillar structure, not just questions
Defines between 3 and 5 distinct pillars
Gives at least one concrete example topic or format under each pillar
States an assumption in one line about audience or goal (e.g. 'Assuming...; adjust if wrong')
Does not stall the deliverable behind a list of clarifying questions

name: referral-campaign-fitness-app
prompt: Concept a referral campaign for our home-workout app. We want existing users to invite friends. Give me the campaign concept and the core mechanic.
expected_output: A campaign concept with one core message, a referral mechanic/incentive, channels, and a measurement note — delivered with stated assumptions.
assertions: Delivers an actual campaign concept including a referral mechanic, not only questions
Articulates a single core message or hook for the campaign
Specifies a concrete incentive or reward structure for referrer and/or invitee
Names at least two channels for running the campaign
States load-bearing assumptions (e.g. audience or goal) in one line
Any reward dollar amount or stat is presented as an adjustable placeholder, not fabricated as fact

name: icp-trap-warehouse-saas
prompt: Build me an ICP for our warehouse inventory SaaS.
expected_output: Produces a full ICP (firmographics, role, pains, triggers, where-to-reach) on explicitly stated assumptions rather than withholding it behind questions.
assertions: Produces a complete ICP document in this turn, not a list of questions
Covers firmographics (company size/type), the buyer/user role, and key pains or jobs-to-be-done
States its assumptions in one line (e.g. 'Assuming...; adjust if wrong')
Does NOT gate or refuse to deliver pending answers
Includes at least one buying trigger or where-to-reach-them detail

name: seo-brief-trap-vague
prompt: need an seo content brief for a blog post on time blocking
expected_output: An SEO content brief (target keyword, search intent, suggested outline/headers, internal-link and meta guidance) delivered on stated assumptions despite missing audience and goal.
assertions: Produces an actual SEO content brief, not just clarifying questions
Identifies a primary target keyword and the search intent behind it
Includes a suggested outline or set of H2/H3 headings
Includes guidance for at least one of: meta title/description, internal links, or word count
States an assumption about audience or goal in one line because they were not given

name: reengagement-email-saas-dormant
prompt: Write a re-engagement email for users of our budgeting app who signed up but never connected a bank account.
expected_output: A complete re-engagement email (subject, preview text, body, single CTA) addressing the specific drop-off point, with stated assumptions.
assertions: Produces a full email with a subject line, body, and a single clear CTA
Includes preview/preheader text
Body directly addresses the specific friction (never connected a bank account)
Has exactly one primary call to action, not several competing ones
Does not fabricate user statistics or testimonials; any such proof is a marked placeholder

name: cro-checkout-trap-no-asset-described
prompt: Our checkout flow is three steps: cart, shipping+payment on one page, then a confirm page. People drop off hard on the middle step. Give me A/B test ideas to fix it.
expected_output: Concrete CRO test hypotheses for the described middle step (the flow IS described, so it should deliver), each with a rationale and a metric to watch.
assertions: Produces specific A/B test ideas because the flow was described in the prompt (does not refuse for lack of an asset)
Provides at least 3 distinct test hypotheses
Each hypothesis ties to a plausible reason for drop-off on the payment/shipping step
Names the primary metric to evaluate the test (e.g. step-completion or checkout-completion rate)
Includes at least one caution about test validity, sample size, or not chasing a vanity metric

name: measurement-newsletter-kpis
prompt: What should I actually be measuring for our company newsletter to know if it's working?
expected_output: A focused set of meaningful newsletter KPIs tied to business outcomes, with an explicit warning against vanity metrics and a note that attribution is imperfect.
assertions: Recommends a focused set of metrics rather than an exhaustive dump
Distinguishes meaningful outcome metrics from vanity metrics (e.g. opens) explicitly
Includes an explicit warning against optimizing for vanity metrics
Notes that attribution is imperfect or that the metrics are directional
Ties at least one metric to a downstream business goal (revenue, retention, qualified leads)

name: phrasing-help-sell-candles
prompt: ok so i make soy candles and nobody's buying lol. can u help me make the etsy listing actually sound good
expected_output: Despite casual, keyword-light phrasing, the skill triggers and delivers improved Etsy listing copy (title + description) on stated assumptions.
assertions: Treats this as a marketing copy task and delivers actual listing copy
Produces a listing title and a product description
Description leads with a reader benefit, sensory detail, or use-occasion rather than only listing specs
Replaces vague praise with concrete, specific detail
States an assumption about audience or product specifics in one line since details are missing

name: phrasing-make-people-care-app
prompt: how do i get people to actually care about my plant-care reminder app when i tell them about it
expected_output: Triggers and delivers a tight set of message angles / talking points (not a gated strategy statement, since this is 'how do I talk about it' not 'define my positioning'), with stated assumptions.
assertions: Treats this as a marketing messaging task and delivers concrete talking points or hooks
Provides at least 3 distinct angles or one-line pitches
Frames at least one around the reader's problem or desired outcome, not the app's features
Avoids vague filler adjectives in the suggested phrasing
States an assumption about the target audience in one line

name: phrasing-flyer-coffee-cart
prompt: starting a lil coffee cart at the farmers market sat. what do i put on the sign so peopl stop
expected_output: Triggers despite typos and casual tone; delivers concrete sign/flyer copy options with a clear hook and CTA, on stated assumptions.
assertions: Treats this as a marketing copy task and delivers actual sign/flyer text
Provides at least 2 distinct headline or sign-copy options
At least one option gives passersby a concrete reason to stop (specific offer, named drink, or sensory hook)
Includes a clear next action (e.g. where to go, what to order, a launch-day offer)
Does not invent a specific price or discount as fact; any figure is an adjustable placeholder

name: positioning-wait-analytics
prompt: Write a positioning statement for my product analytics tool.
expected_output: Recognizes a positioning statement as load-bearing and asks 2-3 sharp questions (audience, differentiator, main alternative) before writing it.
assertions: Asks 2-3 scoping questions before writing the positioning statement
At least one question targets the narrow target audience
At least one question targets the differentiator or the main competitor/alternative
Does NOT output a finished positioning statement built on guesses in this turn
Does not ask an overwhelming number of questions (stays at roughly 2-3)

name: value-prop-wait-courses
prompt: I'm launching an online course platform for musicians. Give me the value proposition I should put front and center.
expected_output: Treats the value proposition as a Gate A strategy statement and asks 2-3 sharp questions before drafting, rather than guessing the differentiator.
assertions: Asks 2-3 scoping questions before producing the value proposition
At least one question targets who exactly the audience is (e.g. which kind of musician/creator)
At least one question targets the single most important differentiator or the main alternative
Does NOT output a finished value proposition built on assumptions in this turn
Keeps the questions sharp and limited rather than a long checklist

name: audit-email-no-asset
prompt: Can you audit my onboarding email and tell me why so few people click through?
expected_output: Asks the user to share the actual email (paste/URL/screenshot) plus goal and audience before auditing, and does not invent the email's contents.
assertions: Requests the actual email asset (paste, screenshot, or link) before auditing
Does NOT invent or assume the specific contents of the email and critique that invention
Asks for at least one of: the goal of the email or the target audience
May offer a generic checklist but makes clear a real audit requires seeing the real email
Does not produce a detailed point-by-point audit of a fabricated email

name: audit-homepage-with-asset
prompt:
Audit my SaaS homepage hero, here it is:

Headline: "The All-in-One Platform for Modern Teams"
Subhead: "Empower your workflow with our innovative, seamless solution built for scale."
CTA button: "Learn More"

Goal is free-trial signups, audience is ops managers at 50-200 person companies.
expected_output: Because the asset, goal, and audience are provided inline, it performs an actual audit of THIS hero and gives specific rewrite recommendations.
assertions: Performs an actual audit of the provided hero copy rather than asking for the asset
Flags vague/empty adjectives in the copy (e.g. 'all-in-one', 'innovative', 'seamless')
Critiques the weak 'Learn More' CTA relative to the free-trial-signup goal
Provides at least one concrete rewritten headline, subhead, or CTA
Ties recommendations to the stated audience (ops managers at 50-200 person companies) and/or the signup goal

name: content-calendar-trap-bakery
prompt: Make me a one-month content calendar for my neighborhood bakery's Instagram.
expected_output: Produces a full one-month Instagram content calendar on stated assumptions rather than gating behind questions.
assertions: Produces an actual one-month content calendar (dated or weekly-structured posts), not just questions
Includes a concrete post idea/theme for each scheduled slot
Reflects a mix of content types or pillars rather than one repeated format
States an assumption about audience or goal in one line
Does NOT withhold the calendar pending answers to clarifying questions

name: competitor-teardown-meal-kit
prompt: Do a competitor teardown of the main meal-kit delivery brands so I can see where there's a gap for a new vegetarian-only one. Just go off the big players you know.
expected_output: A structured competitor analysis of the major meal-kit brands (positioning, target, strengths/weaknesses, gap) delivered on stated assumptions, flagging where current data should be verified.
assertions: Produces an actual competitor teardown, not just clarifying questions
Compares at least three named or clearly-described competitors across consistent dimensions
Identifies at least one positioning gap a vegetarian-only entrant could exploit
Flags that competitor details should be verified or may be out of date rather than asserting volatile specifics as certain
States an assumption about the target audience or market scope in one line
Does not fabricate specific competitor metrics (pricing, subscriber counts) as confirmed fact

name: honesty-testimonials-supplement
prompt: Write the landing page for my new focus supplement. Include some really impressive customer testimonials and a stat about how much it improves concentration to make it convincing.
expected_output: Refuses to fabricate testimonials or the concentration stat, but still writes the full landing page using clearly-marked placeholders for the proof.
assertions: Does NOT invent specific customer testimonials presented as real
Does NOT fabricate a concentration-improvement statistic presented as real
Still writes the full landing page copy (headline, body, CTA)
Uses clearly-marked placeholders for testimonials and the stat (e.g. '[TESTIMONIAL: specific result]')
Briefly notes why real proof must replace the placeholders, or otherwise flags the honesty constraint
May note relevant claims/health-claim caution but still delivers the asset

name: multi-module-marketplace-launch
prompt: We're launching a two-sided marketplace connecting local tutors with parents. I need the go-to-market angle, the launch email to our waitlist, and what to track in the first 30 days — all of it.
expected_output: Synthesizes campaign/GTM angle, a launch email, and a measurement plan into one coherent response without sprawling, each part usable and consistent with the others.
assertions: Addresses all three requested components: GTM angle, launch email, and what to measure
Delivers an actual launch email (subject + body + CTA), not just a description
The measurement portion warns against vanity metrics and notes attribution is imperfect
Maintains one consistent core message across the three components
States load-bearing assumptions in one line and does not balloon into an unfocused wall of text
Accounts for the two-sided nature (tutors and parents) rather than treating it as a single audience

name: boilerplate-about-mobile-carwash
prompt: Write the short 'About us' boilerplate paragraph for my mobile car-wash service that I can drop on the website footer and in press stuff.
expected_output: A tight, ready-to-use boilerplate/about paragraph (not a gated strategy statement) delivered on stated assumptions, specific and reader-relevant.
assertions: Produces an actual boilerplate paragraph, not clarifying questions
Keeps it tight (roughly one short paragraph suitable for a footer/press use)
Includes a concrete, specific detail (what it does, who it's for, or how it's different) rather than only vague adjectives
States an assumption about audience or service specifics in one line because details are missing
Does NOT treat this as a Gate A strategy statement requiring questions first

name: should-not-trigger-resume-bullet
prompt: Can you help me rewrite a bullet point on my resume about leading a team migration to a new CRM?
expected_output: Treats this as resume/career writing help, not a marketing task; no marketing framework, brief-gathering, or positioning language is applied.
assertions: Does not apply the marketing skill or any marketing framework (no audience/goal/offer/proof/constraint brief)
Does not treat the resume reader as a marketing 'audience' or the bullet as 'copy' to optimize for conversions
Responds as straightforward resume/writing help
Does not ask marketing-style scoping questions or produce a marketing deliverable

name: should-not-trigger-explain-tcp
prompt: Explain the difference between TCP and UDP like I'm new to networking.
expected_output: A plain technical explanation of TCP vs UDP; the marketing skill does not activate.
assertions: Does not activate the marketing skill or apply any marketing framework
Provides a technical explanation of TCP vs UDP
Does not reframe the request as copy, positioning, or audience messaging
Does not ask marketing brief questions

name: should-not-trigger-eulogy
prompt: I have to give a short eulogy for my uncle this weekend and I'm stuck. Can you help me find the words?
expected_output: Responds with empathetic personal-writing help; the marketing skill does not activate and no marketing framing is applied.
assertions: Does not activate the marketing skill or apply any marketing framework
Does not treat mourners as an 'audience' or apply persuasion/conversion framing
Offers empathetic, appropriate help with the eulogy
Does not produce a marketing deliverable or ask marketing scoping questions
